metadata.title: Consumer Credit Legislation
metadata.subtitle: Know Your Rights When Borrowing, Disputing Errors, and Dealing with Collectors

content.title: Consumer Credit Legislation
content.subtitle: Know Your Rights When Borrowing, Disputing Errors, and Dealing with Collectors

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
Marcus discovered an incorrect debt of $3,500 on his credit report—a medical bill from a hospital he never visited.

A debt collector has been calling him at 11 PM demanding payment.

When he applied for an apartment, he was denied without explanation.

What laws protect Marcus in this situation, and what steps can he take to resolve these issues?

objectives.description: Identify the major consumer credit protection laws and the specific rights they provide.
Apply appropriate laws to real-world consumer credit scenarios.
Understand how to exercise your rights when dealing with creditors, credit bureaus, and debt collectors.
Take action to dispute errors, stop harassment, and file complaints when your rights are violated.

terms.definition: Requires lenders to clearly disclose APR, total costs, and all loan terms BEFORE you sign. No hidden fees allowed.
terms.example: A car dealer must show you the APR, monthly payment, and total cost before you sign the loan.

terms.definition: Gives you the right to free annual credit reports, to dispute errors, and requires bureaus to investigate within 30 days.
terms.example: You can get free reports at AnnualCreditReport.com and dispute any inaccurate information.

terms.definition: Prohibits debt collector harassment: no calls before 8 AM or after 9 PM, no threats, no contacting your employer about debt.
terms.example: A collector calling at 11 PM or threatening to tell your boss is breaking federal law.

terms.definition: Prohibits credit discrimination based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age, or public assistance.
terms.example: Denying credit because someone is a 'single woman' or 'too old' violates ECOA.

leftPanel.title: Historical Abuses That Led to Laws
paragraphs: Before these protections:
Lenders hid true costs: Borrowers didn't know real APR until too late
Credit bureaus reported errors: No way to dispute inaccurate information
Discrimination was common: Women needed husband's signature, minorities denied credit
Collectors used harassment: Calls at all hours, threats, public shaming
These laws level the playing field between consumers and financial institutions.

infoCard.title: Your Rights Give You Power
items: Know the true cost before borrowing
See and correct your credit reports

leftPanel.title: Disclosure Requirements
paragraphs: Before you sign ANY loan, lenders MUST tell you:
Annual Percentage Rate (APR): True cost including fees
Finance charge: Total dollar amount of interest
Amount financed: What you're actually borrowing
Total of payments: Everything you'll pay over the loan life
Payment schedule: How much and when
This must be in writing, clear language, before signing.

highlightBox.text: If a fee wasn't disclosed before signing, you may have grounds to challenge it under TILA.

stats.label: Right to cancel certain loans (home equity)

leftPanel.title: Your Credit Report Rights
paragraphs: The FCRA gives you powerful rights over your credit information:
Free annual reports: From each bureau (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) at AnnualCreditReport.com
Dispute inaccurate information: Bureaus must investigate within 30 days
Know who's checking: See 'inquiries' on your report
Limited reporting time: Most negative items must be removed after 7 years (bankruptcies: 10 years)
Adverse action notice: If denied credit, you must be told why

stats.label: Most negative items stay on report

layoutData.title: What Debt Collectors CANNOT Do
paragraphs: The FDCPA protects you from harassment and abuse by third-party debt collectors:
bulletPoints: No calls before 8 AM or after 9 PM in your time zone
No harassment: Repeated calls, threats of violence, obscene language
No false statements: Can't lie about amount owed or threaten arrest for debt
No public disclosure: Can't tell your employer, neighbors, or post on social media
No unfair practices: Can't add unauthorized fees or threaten to take property they can't take
Must honor written cease contact requests: If you send a letter saying stop, they must stop (except to confirm)

keyPoint.text: ⚠️ Note: FDCPA applies to third-party collectors, not the original creditor. Different rules may apply.

leftColumn.title: CANNOT Consider
items: Age (if you're old enough to contract)

leftPanel.title: Specific Credit Card Protections
paragraphs: The CARD Act added important protections for credit card users:
45-day notice: Before raising interest rates
No rate increases in first year: Except for variable rates or promotional periods ending
Payments applied fairly: To highest-rate balance first
Clear due dates: Same date each month, reasonable time to pay
No fees for paying by phone: If that's only expedited option
Under-21 protections: Need cosigner or proof of income

infoCard.title: Also Banned:
items: Retroactive rate increases on existing balances

layoutData.title: Taking Action When Rights Are Violated
paragraphs: Knowing your rights is only useful if you act on them:
bulletPoints: Dispute credit report errors: File online, by mail, or phone with each bureau showing the error. Include documentation.
Stop debt collector harassment: Send a written cease contact letter by certified mail. Keep a copy.
Request adverse action explanation: If denied credit, you have 60 days to request specific reasons.
File complaints: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB), Federal Trade Commission (FTC), state attorney general
Document everything: Keep records of calls, letters, and violations—dates, times, what was said

keyPoint.text: 📝 Written documentation is your best protection. Keep copies of everything!

scenario.name: Marcus's Three Problems
paragraphs: Problem 1: A $3,500 debt on his credit report from a hospital he never visited.
Problem 2: Debt collector calling at 11 PM demanding payment.
Problem 3: Apartment application denied without explanation.

scenario.name: Sophia's Credit Denial
paragraphs: Sophia has excellent credit (780 score) and applied for a credit card. She was denied.
When she asked why, the representative said: 'We don't give cards to single women.'
The representative refused to provide any other explanation.

outcomes.label: Violation 2
outcomes.detail: Failed to provide adverse action notice

outcomes.label: Sophia's Action
outcomes.detail: Document what was said, when, by whom

content.question:
Match each scenario to the law that provides protection:

1. Javier signed a car loan but discovered a $500 fee that was never disclosed.

2. Lena gets 5 calls a day from collectors, including at 10:30 PM, threatening to tell her employer.

3. Derek was denied a loan and told 'we don't lend to people on food stamps.'

Why do you think consumer credit protection laws are necessary?

What might happen without them?

How do these laws change your approach to borrowing and dealing with creditors?

questions.question: Which law requires lenders to disclose the APR before you sign a loan?
Under FDCPA, what hours can debt collectors NOT call you?
You find an error on your credit report. What are the steps to dispute it under FCRA?
A lender denies your application because you're 'too old.' Which law does this violate?

takeaways.title: TILA = Disclosure
takeaways.description: Lenders must tell you APR, total cost, and all terms before you sign. No hidden fees.

takeaways.title: FCRA = Credit Reports
takeaways.description: Free annual reports, right to dispute errors, 30-day investigation requirement.

takeaways.title: FDCPA = Collector Limits
takeaways.description: No calls before 8 AM or after 9 PM, no harassment, no threats, must stop if you request in writing.

takeaways.title: ECOA = No Discrimination
takeaways.description: Credit decisions must be based on creditworthiness, not race, gender, age, or marital status.

main.title: Consumer Rights Case Analysis
main.description: Marcus learned his rights—but can you help others identify violations and take action? Tomorrow you'll analyze real scenarios, match them to the correct laws, and determine the proper steps to resolve each situation.

steps: Analyze scenarios and identify which consumer protection law applies
Match specific rights to the laws that provide them
Outline the steps to dispute a credit report error
Identify debt collector violations and appropriate responses
Determine where to file complaints for different violations

content.tagline: Building Financial Futures, One Lesson at a Time
